summary: It's been a busy few days around here. Reynoldstown finally sees that stalled BeltLine development breaking ground, while the rest of the city deals with the nightmare at Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA agents call out amid the government shutdown. Plus, some good eats coming to Inman Park.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Stalled BeltLine Development Finally Breaking Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development has taken over the 85-unit townhome and condo project from Toll Brothers and crews are on site. It's just a block from the Eastside Trail, so you know this has been a long time coming for the neighborhood.
topStory.body:
You know that empty lot on Chester Avenue that's been staring at you every time you walk the BeltLine? The one that got cleared back in 2021 and then just sat there like a missing tooth? Well, finally some good news. Embry Development has taken over the project from Toll Brothers and actual construction is happening.

The 85-unit development at 195 Chester Avenue, now called Camber Crossing, will have a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos spread across 2 acres. It's literally a block west of the Eastside Trail and just north of Memorial Drive, so the location couldn't be better. The original plans got permitted years ago, but you know how these things go in Atlanta.

Embry's managing partner says they're targeting move-ins starting in spring 2027, which honestly feels optimistic given Atlanta construction timelines, but here's hoping. Reynoldstown has been waiting long enough for this corner to come back to life, especially with all the foot traffic from the BeltLine right there.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site showing early groundwork at the Reynoldstown development near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Opens Next Week
stories.summary: Finally, some ITP housing that won't require selling a kidney. Cottages at Midway is launching with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. It's targeting that sweet spot between apartments and $700k single-family homes that Decatur has become known for. The soft launch starts next week.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices pushing past $700k, the Cottages at Midway project feels like a breath of fresh air. This isn't another apartment complex or luxury townhome development. It's 18 actual standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards and green space, designed to feel more like a neighborhood than a development.

The 2.1-acre site at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road was just sitting there as underused lots, so turning it into accessible homeownership makes a lot of sense. The developers are calling it 'missing middle' housing, and honestly, that's exactly what we need more of around here. Something between cramped apartments and those massive single-family houses that only tech bros can afford.

A soft launch starts next week to drum up interest, which probably means they're trying to gauge demand before setting final prices. Given how quickly anything reasonably priced disappears in Decatur, don't be surprised if these move fast once they hit the market officially.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing layout of 18 houses around courtyards at Cottages at Midway in Decatur

stories.headline: West End's BeltLine Townhomes Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: Those West End townhomes that Ackerman & Co. has been promising are actually looking like real buildings now. GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) at 600 Hopkins Street will have 23 units total, with the first seven hitting the market this spring. It's right along the Westside Trail, so location is solid.
stories.body:
After watching demolition crews clear the site at 600 Hopkins Street a year ago, it's nice to see actual vertical construction happening. Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White, have renamed the project GW Parkside and the first phase of seven townhomes is almost topped out.

This stretch of the Westside Trail has been crying out for more housing options, especially for-sale units which are pretty rare along the BeltLine's western reaches. The 23 townhomes will roll out in phases, with that first batch hitting the market sometime this spring according to the signage on site.

What's interesting is seeing Ackerman expand beyond their office and retail focus at Lee + White into actual residential development. Given how well they've managed that complex's mix of tenants and events, there's reason to be optimistic about how this project will fit into the West End neighborhood.
stories.imageAlt: Construction progress showing townhomes under construction along the BeltLine in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: New restaurant alert: Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez are serving up soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. It's their first brick-and-mortar after doing the pop-up circuit around town.
stories.body:
Finally, Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have a permanent home for their Asian-American comfort food. After making their mark on Atlanta's pop-up scene, Mushi Ni opened Monday in that prime Inman Park spot on Elizabeth Street where you can literally walk off the Freedom Park Trail and grab dinner.

The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and those $25 Peking chicken platters that feed two people. Le worked at some serious restaurants before this (including time with Gordon Ramsay in New York), so the technique is there. But this feels more like the kind of place you'd actually want to eat regularly, not just for special occasions.

They're open for both lunch and dinner, with cocktails that include Thai tea martinis and a tamarind whiskey sour. Given how well their pop-ups went over, and that location right off one of the city's most popular trails, I'd expect this place to find its crowd pretty quickly.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant in Inman Park

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to the eastside. Vela Park Food Works opens mid-April near East Lake Golf Club with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. It's part of that new Vela Park rental community that's been under construction.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid keeps expanding his Atlanta food empire. After success with his other local food halls, he's partnering with the developers of Vela Park (that new rental community near East Lake Golf Club) to open Atlanta's latest food destination.

Vela Park Food Works is slated to open mid-April with six different chef-driven concepts, which is Montwaid's usual approach. Think craft cocktails and roomy outdoor dining rather than your typical mall food court setup. The guy knows how to curate food vendors, so even though we don't have the full tenant list yet, track record suggests this will be worth the trip.

The timing makes sense too, opening right as the weather gets nice and people are looking for new outdoor dining spots. East Lake isn't exactly overflowing with food options, so this could become a real destination for that part of town.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall near East Lake

stories.headline: Airport Hell: TSA Agents Call Out as Lines Hit Four Hours
stories.summary: The government shutdown is absolutely crushing Hartsfield-Jackson. TSA agents are calling out sick at record rates, lines are hitting four hours, and the Trump administration sent ICE agents to help (though they can't actually do security screening). Some travelers are spending the night in line. It's a complete mess.
stories.body:
If you're flying out of Hartsfield-Jackson right now, just don't. Or if you absolutely have to, maybe bring a sleeping bag. The partial government shutdown that started in mid-February has turned the world's busiest airport into an absolute nightmare, with TSA agents working without pay and calling out at unprecedented rates.

Nearly 3,500 TSA agents missed work Monday alone, the highest rate since the shutdown began. Wait times are hitting four hours regularly, with some travelers literally spending the night in security lines. The airport is telling people to arrive four hours early, which is insane but probably necessary. ICE agents are on site to help with general security, but they can't actually screen passengers, so they're mostly just standing around.

The ripple effects are hitting everything from business travel to spring break plans. Some folks are driving to Birmingham or Nashville instead, which tells you everything about how bad it's gotten. This is what happens when essential workers get used as political pawns in budget fights.
stories.imageAlt: Long security lines at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport during government shutdown

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls After Firefighter Contract Fight
stories.summary: Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions to limit ICE's presence in the city. They'd ban ICE detention facilities and require documentation when ICE agents show up during police operations. Meanwhile, residents are still mad at Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' union contract that council already approved.
stories.body:
The Public Safety Committee had a lot on its plate Monday, with ICE control measures moving forward while residents continued to blast Mayor Dickens over the firefighters' union contract. The committee unanimously advanced resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and force Atlanta police to document whenever ICE agents are present during their operations.

These measures head to the full council for an April 20 vote, and they're clearly a response to the increased ICE presence we're seeing around town, including at the airport. The documentation requirement is particularly smart, creating an accountability trail for when and how federal immigration enforcement intersects with local policing.

But the bigger drama was residents showing up to demand answers about why Dickens won't sign the firefighter contract that council ratified almost a year ago. It's Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union contract, and the mayor's refusal to sign it is creating a weird legal limbo that's got everyone frustrated. Council can't force him to sign, but the political pressure is clearly building.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking about ICE policies and firefighter contracts

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb
stories.summary: Tragic news from Ellenwood this morning. A 7-year-old boy died after accidentally shooting himself at a home on Loveless Drive. Family members told reporters the child found a gun and it went off. DeKalb police are investigating, but it looks like another preventable tragedy involving unsecured firearms around kids.
stories.body:
Another heartbreaking reminder about gun safety around children. DeKalb police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood early Wednesday morning and found a 7-year-old boy dead from a gunshot wound. Family members at the scene said the child was playing when he found a gun and it discharged.

This is every parent's worst nightmare, and unfortunately it's not uncommon. Kids find guns, kids play with guns, and tragedies happen when firearms aren't properly secured. The details are still being investigated, but this appears to be what police are calling a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon.'

The family and neighbors were clearly devastated, with crowds gathering as word spread through the community. It's another stark reminder that if you have guns in a house with children, they need to be locked up separately from ammunition, period. No exceptions.
stories.imageAlt: DeKalb County police investigating scene of accidental shooting involving child

stories.headline: World Cup Prep Is Pushing Black Residents Out of Their Homes
stories.summary: Capital B has a powerful investigation into how Atlanta's World Cup infrastructure projects are displacing longtime Black residents. From Rodney Cook Sr. Park to other flood control projects, the city's climate investments are pricing out the very communities they're supposed to protect. It's 'climate gentrification' in action.
stories.body:
This is required reading if you care about what's really happening in Atlanta's Black neighborhoods. Capital B's investigation shows how the city's billions in World Cup infrastructure and climate resilience projects are systematically displacing longtime Black residents who've been in these communities for decades.

Take 83-year-old Alfred Tucker, who stood in Rodney Cook Sr. Park pointing toward downtown, watching his Vine City neighborhood transform around him. The park itself is beautiful and addresses real flooding issues, but the ripple effects of all this investment are pushing out people who've called these areas home their whole lives. Property taxes go up, rents increase, and suddenly families can't afford to stay.

It's what researchers call 'climate gentrification' and Atlanta is a textbook case. The city needs flood control and infrastructure improvements, especially with climate change making storms more intense. But when those improvements come without protections for existing residents, you end up with resilient infrastructure in neighborhoods that no longer house the people who used to live there.
stories.imageAlt: Alfred Tucker standing at Rodney Cook Sr. Park pointing toward downtown Atlanta
